edges.description: Impaired late 60S maturation reduces productive ribosome assembly and translation.
edges.source: Defective EFL1-mediated EIF6 release during ribosomal large subunit maturation

edges.description: Hematopoietic output is vulnerable to the translation defect.
intermediate_mechanisms: Ribosomal stress in hematopoietic progenitors and bone marrow failure.

edges.description: Exocrine pancreatic function is impaired downstream of the ribosomopathy.
intermediate_mechanisms: Ribosomal stress in pancreatic exocrine tissue.

edges.description: Myeloid output is commonly reduced within the hematopoietic phenotype.
intermediate_mechanisms: Bone marrow dysfunction and impaired myeloid production.

edges.description: Reduced protein synthesis contributes to impaired somatic growth.
intermediate_mechanisms: Malnutrition, endocrine manifestations, and skeletal growth-plate involvement.

edges.description: Skeletal development is perturbed as part of the SDS2 phenotype.
intermediate_mechanisms: Skeletal dysplasia and abnormal growth during ribosomopathy.

edges.description: The EFL1 ribosomopathy belongs to the SDS leukemia-predisposition spectrum with risk for MDS or AML.
intermediate_mechanisms: Ribosomal stress, marrow failure, and clonal hematopoietic evolution.

edges.description: The selected milder EFL1 genotype still impairs 80S ribosome assembly and leaves residual ribosomopathy.
intermediate_mechanisms: Partial clonal rescue with persistent abnormal 80S ribosome assembly.
edges.source: Bone marrow-specific somatic UPD and partial EFL1 clonal rescue
Pancreatic enzyme replacement or supplementation therapy

edges.target: Bone marrow-specific somatic UPD and partial EFL1 clonal rescue
Defective EFL1-mediated EIF6 release during ribosomal large subunit maturation

nodes.description: Bone marrow-restricted somatic uniparental disomy of chromosome 15 can skew EFL1 allele balance toward a relatively milder pathogenic allele. This is a somatic hematopoietic adaptation rather than a germline causal gene item, and the rescued clone can still retain impaired 80S ribosome assembly.
nodes.id: Bone marrow-specific somatic UPD and partial EFL1 clonal rescue

evidence.reference_title: Somatic uniparental disomy mitigates the most damaging EFL1 allele combination in Shwachman-Diamond syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Here we present 3 unrelated Korean SDS patients who carry biallelic pathogenic variants in EFL1 with biased allele frequencies, resulting from a bone marrow-specific somatic uniparental disomy in chromosome 15.

evidence.reference_title: Somatic uniparental disomy mitigates the most damaging EFL1 allele combination in Shwachman-Diamond syndrome.
evidence.snippet: The loss of EFL1 resulted in a pronounced inhibition of terminal oligopyrimidine element-containing ribosomal protein transcript 80S assembly.

nodes.description: Defective EFL1 function compromises mature ribosome formation and reduces overall protein synthesis, providing a mechanistic basis for the tissue-specific pancreatic, hematopoietic, growth, and skeletal phenotype.

evidence.reference_title: Shwachman-Diamond syndrome due to biallelic EFL1 variants with complex and fatal clinical course in early infancy.
evidence.snippet: Functional analysis of patient-derived B-lymphoblastoid and SV40-transformed fibroblast cell lines suggests that the compound heterozygous EFL1 variants impaired mature ribosome formation leading to compromised protein synthesis, ultimately resulting in a severe form of Shwachman-Diamond syndrome.

nodes.description: Persistent or intermittent neutropenia is a common early hematologic feature across Shwachman-Diamond syndrome, including the EFL1-associated recessive subtype.

evidence.snippet: In almost all affected children, persistent or intermittent neutropenia is an early finding.

nodes.description: EFL1 encodes the elongation factor-like GTPase that cooperates with SBDS in the final cytoplasmic maturation step of the 60S ribosomal subunit. Disease variants impair EIF6 eviction from pre-60S particles, blocking normal late ribosomal large-subunit maturation.
nodes.id: Defective EFL1-mediated EIF6 release during ribosomal large subunit maturation

evidence.reference_title: Mutations in EFL1, an SBDS partner, are associated with infantile pancytopenia, exocrine pancreatic insufficiency and skeletal anomalies in aShwachman-Diamond like syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Similar to the molecular pathology of SDS, mutant EFL1 proteins do not promote the release of cytoplasmic Tif6 from the 60S subunit, likely preventing the formation of mature ribosomes.

evidence.reference_title: EFL1 mutations impair eIF6 release to cause Shwachman-Diamond syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Cellular defects in these individuals include impaired ribosomal subunit joining and attenuated global protein translation as a consequence of defective eIF6 eviction.

evidence.reference_title: EFL1 mutations impair eIF6 release to cause Shwachman-Diamond syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Here, we report biallelic mutations in EFL1 in 3 unrelated individuals with clinical features of SDS.

nodes.description: Exocrine pancreatic insufficiency is a core gastrointestinal feature of EFL1-related Shwachman-Diamond syndrome.

evidence.reference_title: Mutations in EFL1, an SBDS partner, are associated with infantile pancytopenia, exocrine pancreatic insufficiency and skeletal anomalies in aShwachman-Diamond like syndrome.
evidence.snippet: This study aims to identify the molecular basis of an SDS-like disease, manifested by pancytopenia, exocrine pancreatic insufficiency and skeletal abnormalities in six patients from three unrelated families.

nodes.description: Granulocyte colony-stimulating factor pharmacotherapy can be used for severe recurrent infection with persistent severe neutropenia in the SDS spectrum, while prolonged cytokine or hematopoietic growth-factor exposure should be approached cautiously.

evidence.snippet: If recurrent infections are severe and absolute neutrophil counts are persistently ≤500/mm3, treatment with granulocyte-colony stimulation factor (G-CSF) can be considered and may be especially helpful when interventions such as complex dental procedures or orthopedic surgery are being considered.
Prolonged use of cytokine and hematopoietic growth factors (e.g., G-CSF) should be considered with caution.

nodes.description: Serial complete blood counts with differential and platelet count, together with periodic bone marrow examination, monitor cytopenias, marrow failure, MDS, and AML risk in the SDS spectrum.

evidence.snippet: Complete blood count with white blood cell differential and platelet count at least every three to six months; bone marrow examination every one to three years or more frequently if bone marrow changes are observed.

nodes.description: Metaphyseal dysplasia expands the recognized skeletal phenotype of EFL1-related disease and supports separation from classic SBDS-related SDS at the level of case-based phenotype curation.

evidence.reference_title: Further evidence for the involvement of EFL1 in a Shwachman-Diamond-like syndrome and expansion of the phenotypic features.
evidence.snippet: We report a pediatric patient who presented with a metaphyseal dysplasia and was found to have biallelic variants in EFL1 on reanalysis of trio whole-exome sequencing data.

nodes.description: Nutritional status and fat-soluble vitamin concentrations should be followed longitudinally because exocrine pancreatic dysfunction can cause malabsorption and malnutrition.

evidence.snippet: Assessment of nutritional status and measurement of serum concentration of fat-soluble vitamins every six months.

nodes.description: Oral pancreatic enzyme replacement with fat-soluble vitamin supplementation is used to manage exocrine pancreatic insufficiency in the SDS spectrum.
nodes.id: Pancreatic enzyme replacement or supplementation therapy

evidence.snippet: Exocrine pancreatic insufficiency is treated with oral pancreatic enzymes and fat-soluble vitamin supplementation.

nodes.description: Infantile pancytopenia is a recurrent hematologic presentation in the initial EFL1-related cohort.

evidence.reference_title: Mutations in EFL1, an SBDS partner, are associated with infantile pancytopenia, exocrine pancreatic insufficiency and skeletal anomalies in aShwachman-Diamond like syndrome.
evidence.snippet: This study aims to identify the molecular basis of an SDS-like disease, manifested by pancytopenia, exocrine pancreatic insufficiency and skeletal abnormalities in six patients from three unrelated families.

nodes.description: EFL1-related Shwachman-Diamond syndrome carries a risk of myeloid malignant transformation, including myelodysplastic syndrome and acute myeloid leukemia, within the broader SDS ribosomopathy spectrum.

evidence.reference_title: EFL1 mutations impair eIF6 release to cause Shwachman-Diamond syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Shwachman-Diamond syndrome (SDS) is a recessive disorder typified by bone marrow failure and predisposition to hematological malignancies.

nodes.description: Short stature is a common growth manifestation within the Shwachman-Diamond syndrome spectrum and is included in the MONDO textual definition of SDS2.

evidence.snippet: Short stature and recurrent infections are common.

nodes.description: Orthopedic imaging during rapid growth stages and regular growth/endocrine assessment are used to monitor skeletal dysplasia, asymmetric growth, and endocrine manifestations.

evidence.snippet: Monitor for orthopedic complications with radiographs of the hips and knees during the most rapid growth stages.
Assess growth and for clinical signs and symptoms of additional endocrine manifestations every six months.
